title: Ghormeh sabzi
subtitle: Persian herb and lamb stew with dried limes
excerpt: Iran's most beloved stew: a dark, fragrant braise of lamb, kidney beans, and an enormous quantity of fried dried herbs (fenugreek, parsley, coriander, and chives) soured with whole dried limes pierced and dropped into the pot. The herbs must be fried first until almost black; this is not a mistake but the technique that gives the stew its characteristic deep flavour.
sourceNotes: Ghormeh sabzi is considered Iran's national dish, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is eaten at Persian New Year (Nowruz) and at family occasions. The combination of fried herbs, dried limes (loomi), and lamb is uniquely Iranian. The recipe varies by family and region in the balance of herbs and the cooking time.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the cooked stew for up to 3 months. Reheat gently — the flavour deepens further.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Ghormeh sabzi is better the day after cooking. Make ahead and refrigerate overnight.

content.text: The herb-frying step is the most important and most counterintuitive part of the recipe. A very large quantity of chopped fresh herbs (parsley, coriander, chives, and dried fenugreek leaves) is fried in olive oil over medium heat for 20 to 25 minutes, stirring regularly, until the herbs are completely dry, dark, and reduced to a fraction of their original volume. They should look almost burnt. This process drives off the water and concentrates the volatile oils, producing the deep, complex flavour that defines ghormeh sabzi. Fresh, barely-wilted herbs produce a completely different dish.

The dried limes (loomi) are pierced several times with a skewer or knife before adding to the stew. This allows the sour juice inside to seep out during cooking. They are not eaten but impart a unique fermented citrus flavour that cannot be replicated with fresh lemon or lime. Serve over plain white rice or alongside tahdig.

items.prepNote: pierced all over with a skewer

content.text: Heat 3 tablespoons of the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a wide pan over medium heat. Add all the chopped herbs ({{parsley-flat}}, {{coriander}}, {{chives}}) and the {{fenugreek-leaves}}. Fry, stirring frequently, for 20 to 25 minutes until the herbs are dark, dry, and reduced to about a quarter of their original volume. They should be almost black. Remove and set aside.
Brown the lamb and build the stew

In a large heavy pot, heat the remaining {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}}. Brown the {{lamb-shoulder}} pieces in batches. Remove. Cook the {{onion}} for 10 minutes until golden. Add the {{turmeric}} and cook for 1 minute. Return the lamb, add 600 ml water, and bring to the boil. Skim, then add the fried herbs and the pierced {{dried-lime}}. Season with salt. Cover and simmer on the lowest possible heat for 1.5 hours.
Add the {{kidney-beans}} in the last 30 minutes of cooking. The sauce should be dark, thick, and coating the meat. Remove the dried limes before serving. Check seasoning.

items.symptom: Stew tastes green and grassy rather than deep and complex
items.fix: The herbs must be fried until almost black — this is the foundational technique and cannot be rushed. 20 minutes is the minimum; 25 is better. The aroma should be nutty and dark, not fresh and bright

items.cause: Too many or cracked and disintegrating
items.fix: Use 3 to 4 limes and pierce rather than crack them. If one splits open during cooking, remove it immediately as the seeds are intensely bitter. Taste the stew regularly

items.symptom: Lamb is still tough after 1.5 hours
items.cause: Pieces too large or heat too low
items.fix: Cut to 4 cm maximum. The simmer must be active, not just a gentle warm. Add 30 minutes more if needed

items.cause: Too many dried limes or they cracked and released their seeds
items.fix: Remove limes and add a teaspoon of sugar. The sourness should be present but not overwhelming — balanced by the richness of the lamb and herbs

content.text: Ghormeh sabzi is the dish that Iranians in the diaspora miss most, and the one most often made for important occasions. Its flavour is completely distinctive: the deeply fried herb base, the sour dried limes, the slow-cooked lamb — and it smells of Iran in the way that certain dishes smell precisely of their country of origin. It is the dish served at Persian New Year, made by mothers and grandmothers in enormous pots, and carried in containers to relatives who live far away. The recipe is intensely personal: every family has their own proportions, their own herb balance, their own opinion on whether to use fresh or dried kidney beans. The version here is a reliable starting point.
